What 67 year old has interest with Minecraft, that’s an obviously expected age group

​@jiggypuffy You do realize that there are many more expected age groups of minecraft? Also there are way more age groups just in general rather than "67 year olds?" Theres infants, kids, teens, young adults, adults, middle aged, elderly, centenerians/supercentenerians god knows how many more. 

Be quiet lil bro.

Young kids, under the age of 11-12 are also a lot into minecraft
Furthermore, these kids would probably come to this movie with their adult parents. There were a few at the theater.
Also, there were a few young adult groups. People aged around 18-25. 


Also, I'm not talking about an extremely surprising point behind specifically that lots of teenage groups were there. That's obvious, but the theater being only 20% full was quite absurd because many people including me are mostly used to packed theaters with pre-booking and many free seats.

⁠@neilgour AN obvious expected age group. Yes, there are indeed parents supervising children, but are they really there just to watch it? I feel like some of them have interest with Minecraft, but some don’t and just prioritize the safety and rules of the theater.
Older siblings definitely would have some interest though and would watch younger children, and friend groups too.
Also, this is not an excuse for my point, but it was MY opinion. I did not go out researching something such as “minecraft age groups”, but im not gonna argue too much about this.

Bueno, llego demasiado tarde para dar mi opinión. 
Veamos, primero que nada, no creo que sea horrible, pero puedo entender por qué muchas personas encontraron los diseños (algunos de ellos) 
extraños y desagradables. Supongo que es la primera reacción que la mayoría tiene al ver a los personajes en "Live Action" — se sienten fuera de lugar.

Segundo, "Minecraft Jumanji" — comparto algunas opiniones en ese punto. 
La historia pudo haber tomado direcciones diferentes, pero me estoy adelantando; todavía no sabemos mucho sobre su desarrollo. 
Aún tengo la esperanza de que nos sorprenda en ese aspecto.

Sé que muchas personas preferían una película animada, pero afortunadamente aún está la serie de Netflix, que ofrece más contenido 
y duración que una película. Por otro lado, están los directores, que ya explicaron por qué eligieron live action en lugar de una película animada.

Mis esperanzas aumentaron con el primer tráiler, y algunas imágenes que vi me convencieron (aún más X)..

Everybody whinging over this basically tells you the emotional maturity of the fanbase.

Oh, a Minecraft movie? Yeah, because nothing says "cinematic masterpiece" like a blocky world and endless hours of crafting. I mean, who wouldn't want to watch a bunch of characters wander around in pixelated landscapes, trying to find meaning in a game that’s about building and destroying… kind of like this movie, really.

The plot? Oh, don’t worry, it’s got more holes than a creeper’s favorite hobby. It’s as if someone decided to mix Lord of the Rings with The Lego Movie but forgot to add the "fun" part.

And the dialogue—brilliant! Who needs character development when you can have endless exposition about “mining” and “crafting”? I can’t wait to hear the emotional monologue about iron ore. “I’ve spent my whole life in this cave, but I must break free. For the diamonds… for the world!”

Also, the special effects? They went all out, you can tell. Nothing screams cutting-edge cinema like watching a character run through a field of square trees, the highest budget in Hollywood. It’s like they took a game made by a 15-year-old with a 2D brain and decided to turn it into a movie no one asked for.

And the soundtrack? It’s got the same vibe as listening to a cat play the piano after it knocked it over. It's got the depth of a dirt block—just like the plot.

Be There and Be Square. #MinecraftMovie​ - only in theaters 2025

From Warner Bros. Pictures and Legendary Pictures and starring Jason Momoa and Jack Black, “A Minecraft Movie,” directed by Jared Hess, is the first-ever big screen, live-action adaptation of Minecraft, the best-selling video game of all time.

The film also stars Emma Myers (“Wednesday”), Oscar nominee Danielle Brooks (“The Color Purple”), Sebastian Eugene Hansen (“Just Mercy, “Lisey’s Story”), with Jennifer Coolidge.

Welcome to the world of Minecraft, where creativity doesn’t just help you craft, it’s essential to one’s survival!  Four misfits—Garrett “The Garbage Man” Garrison (Momoa), Henry (Hansen), Natalie (Myers) and Dawn (Brooks)—find themselves struggling with ordinary problems when they are suddenly pulled through a mysterious portal into the Overworld: a bizarre, cubic wonderland that thrives on imagination.  To get back home, they’ll have to master this world (and protect it from evil things like Piglins and Zombies, too) while embarking on a magical quest with an unexpected, expert crafter, Steve (Black).  Together, their adventure will challenge all five to be bold and to reconnect with the qualities that make each of them uniquely creative…the very skills they need to thrive back in the real world. 

Oscar nominee Hess (“Ninety-Five Senses,” “Nacho Libre”) directed, with Roy Lee, Jon Berg, Mary Parent, Cale Boyter, Jason Momoa, Jill Messick, Torfi Frans Olafsson and Vu Bui producing, and Todd Hallowell, Kayleen Walters, Brian Mendoza, Jonathan Spaihts, Pete Chiappetta, Andrew Lary and Anthony Tittanegro executive producing.

The director’s creative team behind the camera includes BAFTA-nominated director of photography Enrique Chediak (“127 Hours,” “Transformers: Rise of the Beasts”), Oscar-winning production designer Grant Major (“The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King,” “The Meg”), editor James Thomas (“Pokémon: Detective Pikachu”, the “Borat” films), Oscar-winning VFX supervisor Dan Lemmon (“The Jungle Book,” “The Batman”), and costume designer Amanda Neale (“The Meg,” “What We Do in the Shadows”).  Casting is by Rachel Tenner.  The music supervisors are Gabe Hilfer and Karyn Rachtman, and the music is by Mark Mothersbaugh (“Thor: Ragnarok,” the “LEGO®” movies).

Warner Bros. Pictures and Legendary Pictures Present A Vertigo Entertainment/On The Roam/Mojäng Aktiebolag Production, A Jared Hess Film, “A Minecraft Movie.”  The film will be distributed by Warner Bros. Here is why.

1: STEVE IS NOT WHITE. I love Jack Black, but why is he just playing himself?

2: Sheep don’t have top teeth. They didn’t even do any research.

3: the CGI is terrible. The players look completely detached from the world. 

4: piglens. Change. When. They. Go. Into. The . Overword. ANOTHER INSTANCE OF NO RESEARCH. 

5: Jumanji ripoff. 

There are so many problems with this movie but I won’t write them all down, since no one will take the time to read them. 

Minecraft isn’t just a game you can make a crappy movie about for publicity. It’s part of millions of people’s lives. 

Best case scenario: they cancel this movie.

Podría aceptar esta especie de mod con shaders hiperealistas, pero ¿por qué alterar los modelos de los mobs? -Ese cerdo- Esa oveja es espantoso, ¿y por qué hace esa mueca?

Se rumorea que iba a haber una película de Minecraft desde ~2015, y después de ese tiempo lo que ofrecen es otra película de personajes del "mundo real" atrapados en un "mundo ficticio", todo muy original, algo que no hemos visto 500 veces antes, ¿no?

Y en cuanto a esos personajes del "mundo real", por lo que se muestra en este teaser, no parece que se esten tomando en serio la situación, por lo que probablemente la película sea una comedia, pero por los gestos exagerados, me transmite que el tono de la película sera el de una mala comedia.

Y por último, veo que los malos son una clase de hombres cerdo Hace tiempo que no juego Minecraft, pero ¿hombres cerdo? ¿donde estan los zombies, esqueletos, arañas y endermans?

------------------------------------

Más que una película de Minecraft, esto parece un capítulo de una serie, donde los protagonistas se ponen a jugar un juego sospechosamente similar a Minecraft, solo porque los responsables decidieron aprovechar su moda.

To Minecraft: Story Mode.
From the deepest depths of my humble heart, perhaps even under such a spot; I am most sincerely sorry for whatever that so-called Minecraft “movie” is. Warner Bros. Pictures don’t even know what the Minecraft fans truly want. If only they had learned from you and made a fully animated adaptation of a Minecraft movie, this situation would’ve been totally different. After watching that failure of a trailer it had, I have fully realised that I definitely treated you very harshly. I laughed at the animations of the characters that I perceived poor, not realising that a much even worse counterpart of that would’ve been created in the future. I do not expect to be forgiven, and I would definitely move on whenever I say the specific words of you not forgiving me. But I sincerely hope that you would accept this apology, even if only for the slightest bit of time, insignificant to the grand scheme of things. Again, I am most sincerely sorry.

Unprecedentedly hopelessly and regretfully,
from your new, unseen fan,
Moses C.

This live action thing could work if it wasn’t just the cast as real people. Instead, it should be all realistic and not blocky. Otherwise everything needs to be blocky, aka animated. 

Two choices with this realistic look, first option is to animate the cast into blocky animated characters like the rest (just don’t make it look like another ugly Sonic situation), or second option, completely redo the environments into a totally realistic setting. That is, make everything else live action too, and not animated. Still trying to keep the Minecraft vibe of course.

Or at least animate Steve. At least depending on the reason for and why the people aren’t animated, I guess Steve not being animated is okay. To me Steve seems more Minecraft linked like the mobs are. The others from the outer/real world might be okay. Still probably best to animate them as well though.

And as always, there is a third option which is to abandon realistic designs altogether and just do the typical Minecraft designs with animated blocky characters. Maybe non-blocky could work if animated, but this takes might be done best with some testing first if it hasn’t been done already. I’m doubtful it would work since as mentioned before, it’s a clear distinction between Minecraft’s blocky environment and cartoon style design.

Here, everything just looks out of place due to real people contrasted with the very very clearly animated blocky backgrounds and mobs/characters. 

Hopes this helps. Nothing wrong with delaying the movie for a redesign.

I’ve played Minecraft for over 10 years, and THIS is what we all get as the movie?!?! I get it WB, Jack Black is a good actor, but why is Jack Black playing the part of “Steve” of ALL the actors in Hollywood WHY HIM!?!? And what’s up with the seemingly almost “Jumanji” feel to it? The CGI is abhorrently disgusting, and don’t let me forget about what’s with the other 4 characters?! Why is Jason Mamoa (who if I had the choice, swap him and Jack as Steve) in it? Where is Alex? Why are the Piglins the “main threat” in the film? What’s with the disgusting cgi for the animals? Why is this movie done the way it is?!?! You guys had so much source material and used barely any! Why isn’t it animated the same way that Minecraft: Story Mode is? 
Get rid of Jack Black, have Jason Mamoa voice Steve. Put Alex in the movie. 
No, actually recast Steve. Get Danial Radcliffe to play Steve. 
In other words, we don’t need every single movie adaptation of a video game to be live action. Take a look the The Super Mario Bros. movie. That was 100% animated and was a million times better than its live action interpretation from 1993. 
Minecraft: Movie Edition (as it should be called) is not meant to be live action, it’s meant to be animated. 
Side note: Just because you hire A list actors, doesn’t automatically mean people are going to go see it or even like the movie. Not to mention the whole controversy with Jack Black and Tenacious D.

Do you guys really wanna see the Minecraft movie?

There is one thing I like about this. This movie isn't trying to step on the shoes of any fan creations. Live action actors in high budget CGI settings is something only a big studio like Warner Bros could do and it means this movie isn't going to be like any other Minecraft movie. Which means every Minecraft movie holds their own unique and creative value. Look at Parkour Civilization and how that tells a beautiful story with the machinima style. If the official Minecraft movie was built on all the fan movies, those fan movies would have lost some value. I think this is a beautiful way to keep the spotlight on those fan creations, all while inspiring and bringing a community together to make their own versions of this trailer. Now, if only the actors weren't giants. I'm pretty sure Jason Mamoa is 12ft tall and that kid is 7ft. Then there's the AI art in trailer 3...

Dear FNAF Movie Team

I hope this message finds you well. I am writing to express my sincere apologies for my premature judgment of the FNAF movie trailer. In my initial reaction, I may have let my personal expectations overshadow the creativity and effort that went into bringing this beloved franchise to the big screen.

Upon reflection, I realize that every film faces its own unique challenges, and it can be easy to lose sight of the hard work and dedication that goes into each production. Recently, I watched the trailer for the upcoming Minecraft movie, which, while highly anticipated, seemed to lack the same level of depth and attention to detail that the FNAF movie trailer showcased. This has made me reconsider my previous opinions, and I now see that my initial reaction was unfair.

The FNAF franchise has a passionate and loyal fanbase, and the team behind this film has undoubtedly poured their heart and soul into making it resonate with both longtime fans and newcomers alike. I appreciate all the creativity and effort that goes into adapting such a complex and intricate story for the screen.

I understand that my judgment can impact how others perceive the film, and for that, I am truly sorry. I will certainly approach the FNAF movie—and any future trailers—with a more open mind and a greater appreciation for the work involved.

Thank you for your understanding.

Warm regards,

Vybez

The animation is also horrifying, and even worse is that it's paired with real life. They could at the very least, stick to one thing and be consistent. Additionally, I love Jack Black, but he isn't physically accurate to Steve. Maybe he could have voiced him if they did an animated version or something. The whole crafting scene I hated. The items in the back look fake and not size-accurate, plus, what is with an atom of iron crafting two buckets? I mean, seriously, what was that recipe? Did I mention the horrific animation style? It does not suit the mobs at all, it's more like a shader than classic Minecraft. Then again, I'm just a fourteen-year-old against a Warner Bros movie. Still, I feel like the fact that the fanbase seems to be supportive of my perspective as opposed to a film-making industry says something.

Honestly, it’s not that hard to play a video game and decide if a movie adaptation would be better as an animated film or live action. Here are some examples:
Super Mario Bros: Animation; the unique game-style makes that obvious! 
Tomb Raider: Live Action; It’s one of those realistic looking games, so Live Action was the way to go!
Uncharted: Live Action; Treasure Hunting Game? Epic Auests? Realistic graphics? Easily better Live Action!
Ratchet and Clank: Animation; Unique Animation and Unique characters, made the right call here.
Pokémon: Either Way; This is the one case where either works. Detective Pikachu was a great movie, and so were the older animated movies too!
Five Nights at Freddy’s: Live Action; The FNaF games might not look realistic like Tomb Raider and Uncharted do, but the storyline of FNaF and the whole Pizzeria Setting and killer Animatronics vibe would be better Live Action. They definitely made the right call here!
Angry Birds: Animation; There’s birds and pigs that have a unique style/look and they all have superpowers, these movies were definitely better animated then they would’ve been live action!

I think I’ve given enough examples. I personally think it’s common sense to make this an animated movie instead of making it not look like Minecraft at all. Minecraft has a unique style to it which would’ve made this movie great! In my opinion, Warner Bros should cancel this movie and come out with an animated and more accurate Minecraft Movie in a few years.

Expecting a Minecraft movie for some time, I spent years thinking about how it could be done.  I came up with two ideas.  They could do an animation that was fun and funny, I was thinking something Lego Movie like in plot and characterisation but with a look close to the original Minecraft.  The other would be a live action that was perhaps more atmospheric, dark and moody (enhanced by C418 music) and the struggle someone has to survive against the elements and the mobs followed by uncertainty and conflict with people they later meet It might have an almost Robinson Crusoe/ Castaway type plot, but still with elements of Minecraft.  This, however, seems to neither get the atmosphere or the fun of the game.

I saw Minecraft live and the director’s response on why they chose the live action route for this movie. And I have to say, I get it but… how is the movie being in live action different? Animation is not generic! It’s one of the most diverse and unique forms of art! If anything live action is generic and uncreative. Animation has always been the best choice for video game movies and shows. Look at how successful the Mario Movie was! Only in rare cases does live action work, like the Sonic Movie. Which only worked because when the first result was shown people gave it very HARSH criticism. But guess what… they listened and it worked! That’s the only reason why it worked. But if they wanted to do something “different” than do something different with ANIMATION and not to go to “live action because easy” trend. This was a mistake by them.
Can’t wait for the movie to come out and all the comments say “We owe this movie an apology”.

But, in the end, I LEGIT think, it is nice to mix things up a bit and "sometimes" turn something not so realistic into something realistic. Nahhhh this ain’t Minecraft, this is Block Craft 3D: Building Games 😂😭

Over the last 15 yrs, the best Minecraft content has come from animators creating incredible works of art and inspiring influencers such as Technoblade (Technoblade never dies). If you all over at Mojang and Warner Bros truly cared about the community as much as we care about this game coming to life, this live action Jumanji recreation won't happen and something like Villager News will happen. If an animated Minecraft movie came out instead, you would make a lot more money in the box office because new AND old players would go see it. Not just ignorant Moms and Dads taking their 8 yr old to see a movie that doesn't follow the gameplay progression that all players know and love. Like, come on! You have all the tools in front of you to create something amazing and that tool is simply the game of Minecraft and it's history online.

Imagine a movie where a couple players oblivious to how to beat the game figure it all out. Building shelter, fighting mobs, exploring caves and the natural world, finding diamonds and other ores, speaking with villagers and other veteran inhabitants (Steve and possibly Herobrine) to find out more about the world, going to the nether, facing new terrifying landscapes and new monsters, collecting items only spoken about in legends, finding the stronghold and finally fighting the ender dragon!.. And as the exp falls down, the crackling stops, and the portal opens and all players jump in and ending the story with the idea that the game doesn't stop there.. It carries on into real life where you can take the skills you gained in Minecraft such as problem-solving, exploration, teamwork, courage, creativity, compassion and so on to possibly change the world. That's what Minecraft is to me; a creative outlet where dreams are crafted and then built into reality..

I pray you all see this. This game was everything to me as a kid. It helped me through the darkest nights of my life by giving me life long friends and here I am today passionately begging for something that does this game of a lifetime justice. I still reference the end poem because of how impactful it was to my adolescent self. On the surface, it may just be a bunch of blocks and words but this game and its ending screamed:

"Now go and do something in the great and beautiful dream of life! Create something new that will help others around you! And always remember: YOU ARE LOVED!"
